questions.promptTex: \text{After } n = 3 \text{ iterations of the Sierpinski triangle, how many small triangles are drawn?}
hints: Each iteration multiplies the count by 3.

questions.prompt: What happens to the total AREA of the Sierpinski triangle as iterations → ∞?
hints: Each step keeps 3/4 of the previous area.
questions.explanation: Each iteration multiplies area by $3/4$. So area $= (3/4)^n \cdot A_0 \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

questions.prompt: What happens to the total PERIMETER as iterations → ∞?
hints: Each iteration multiplies the perimeter by $3/2$.
questions.explanation: Perimeter scales by $3/2$ each step (3 times as many sides, each at half length). Infinite in the limit.

questions.promptTex: \text{The fractal dimension of the Sierpinski triangle is } \log 3 / \log 2. \\ \text{Round to 3 decimals.}

questions.prompt: If you color Pascal's triangle so odd entries are BLACK and even entries are WHITE, what pattern emerges?
hints: The parity of $\binom{n}{k}$ has fractal structure.
questions.explanation: Coloring Pascal's triangle by the parity of its entries gives a Sierpinski-triangle pattern.

questions.promptTex: \text{At iteration } 5, \text{ how many small triangles are in the Sierpinski triangle?}

questions.prompt: The 'chaos game': start at a random point, repeatedly move halfway toward a random vertex of an equilateral triangle. What emerges?
hints: The dots converge to the Sierpinski triangle.
questions.explanation: Surprisingly, after thousands of iterations, the random points fill out the Sierpinski triangle exactly. This is the simplest example of an 'iterated function system'.

questions.prompt: Fractals like the Sierpinski triangle are described as having:
hints: Zoom in on any piece of a Sierpinski triangle — what do you see?
A smaller copy of the whole thing.
questions.explanation: Self-similarity: each part is a smaller copy of the whole. That's the defining feature of fractals.
